description:
This image displays two maps, (a) and (b), illustrating ground motion measurements (likely Persistent Scatterer Interferometry, PSI, points) for a region in Switzerland, centred around cities like Neuchâtel, Lausanne, and Berne, and including Lac de Neuchâtel. Both maps overlay coloured data points on a grey-scale base map, indicating ground displacement in millimetres (mm).

A common colour legend is provided for both maps, ranging from red for negative values (subsidence) to blue for positive values (uplift), with green indicating stable areas (0 mm):
*   Red to orange: -9 mm to -1 mm (subsidence)
*   Green: 0 mm (stable)
*   Light blue to dark blue: 1 mm to 9 mm (uplift)

Map (a) shows ground motion data where the time series is referred to the primary image acquisition time. This map displays a varied spatial pattern with distinct clusters of red (subsidence) and blue (uplift) points, particularly along linear features resembling infrastructure.
Map (b) presents the same ground motion data but with the time series referred to the first image in the dataset. This map appears shifted, with a higher prevalence of green points indicating values closer to 0 mm.

Both maps include a "5 km" scale bar. The visual difference between (a) and (b) demonstrates how the selection of a temporal reference point for European Ground Motion Service (EGMS) Interferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar (InSAR) time series can introduce a constant shift in the absolute ground motion values, while the underlying relative motion patterns remain consistent.
